description: List all files and directories including hidden ones with permissions, owner, size, and timestamps
notes: Use -la to always include hidden files; bare 'ls' misses dotfiles. For directories only: ls -d */

description: List only subdirectories in the current directory

flags.-d: list directories themselves, not their contents

notes: The trailing slash glob */ expands to only directory entries

description: Print the absolute path of the current working directory
notes: Returns a fully resolved path, resolving symlinks only with pwd -P

description: Change working directory to the current user's home directory
notes: ~ expands to $HOME. Equivalent: 'cd' with no arguments also goes home

description: Switch back to the last directory you were in
notes: cd - prints the path it switches to and toggles between two directories

description: Move to the parent directory
notes: cd ../.. goes up two levels; use pwd afterward to confirm location

description: Create a single new directory with the given name
notes: Fails if directory already exists. Use mkdir -p <NAME> to suppress errors and create parent dirs as needed

description: Create directory and all required parent directories in one command

flags.-p: create parents as needed, no error if existing

notes: e.g. mkdir -p src/components/utils creates three levels at once

description: Create a new empty file, or update the access/modification timestamp if it already exists
notes: Does not overwrite content if the file exists — only updates timestamps

description: Create an empty file with the given name and extension
notes: Extension is purely cosmetic; Linux does not require file extensions

description: Permanently remove a single file — no trash, no undo
notes: DESTRUCTIVE and permanent. Use rm -i for interactive confirmation prompt. Never run rm -rf / or rm -rf *

description: Recursively delete a directory and all its contents
notes: DESTRUCTIVE and permanent. Add -i for per-file confirmation. rmdir works only on empty directories

description: Remove a directory only if it is empty — safer than rm -r
notes: Fails with an error if the directory has any contents, acting as a safety check

description: Copy a file, creating DEST as the new copy
notes: If DEST is an existing directory, the file is copied into it. Use cp -p to preserve timestamps and permissions

description: Recursively copy an entire directory tree
notes: Use cp -rp to also preserve permissions and timestamps

description: Move a file into a directory
notes: Also used to rename: 'mv old.txt new.txt'. Works across filesystems unlike hard links

description: Rename a file or directory by moving it to a new name in the same location
notes: mv overwrites <NEW> if it already exists without warning unless -i is used

description: Print the full contents of a file to stdout
notes: For large files, use 'less <FILENAME>' to paginate. cat also concatenates multiple files: cat file1 file2

description: Open a file in a scrollable pager — q to quit, / to search
notes: Preferred over cat for large files. Supports forward/backward navigation and regex search

description: Print the first N lines of a file
notes: Default without -n is 10 lines. Useful for inspecting file structure without loading the whole file

description: Print the last N lines of a file

flags.-f: follow — stream new lines as they are appended

notes: Use 'tail -f <FILENAME>' to follow a live log file in real time

description: Stream a file in real time, printing new lines as they are written
notes: Ctrl+C to stop. Use tail -F to keep following even if the file is rotated

description: Open a file in the nano text editor (beginner-friendly, Ctrl+O to save, Ctrl+X to exit)
notes: For power users: vim <FILENAME> or vi <FILENAME>. nano is safest default for agents

description: Recursively search for all files matching an extension from the current directory
notes: Use -iname for case-insensitive matching. Scope to a path: find /var/log -name '*.log'

description: Search the entire filesystem for a file by exact name, suppressing permission errors
notes: Searching from / is slow; scope to /home or /var when possible

description: Find files whose modification time is within the last 24 hours
notes: -mtime 1 means 1-2 days ago; -mtime -1 means within the last day (same as 0 for most use cases). Use -newer <REFFILE> to find files newer than a reference file

description: Find all empty subdirectories recursively
notes: find . -type f -empty finds empty files instead

description: Count the number of newline characters (lines) in a file
notes: wc -w <FILENAME> counts words; wc -c <FILENAME> counts bytes. Combine: wc -lwc <FILENAME>

description: Display detailed info including human-readable file size
notes: Use du -sh <FILENAME> for actual disk usage (differs from apparent size for sparse files)

description: Show permissions, owner, group, size, and last-modified date of a file
notes: Permission string format: -rwxr-xr-x (type + owner + group + other)

description: Add execute permission for all users (owner, group, others)
notes: Use chmod u+x <FILENAME> to restrict execute permission to the owner only

description: Set file permissions using an octal mode (e.g. 755, 644, 600)
notes: 755 = rwxr-xr-x (executable, world-readable). 644 = rw-r--r-- (readable, not executable). 600 = rw------- (private)

description: Change the owning user of a file (requires sudo or root)
notes: chown <USER>:<GROUP> <FILENAME> changes both owner and group in one command

description: Create a gzip-compressed tar archive of a directory
notes: Use -j instead of -z for bzip2 (smaller but slower). Verify with: tar -tzf <NAME>.tar.gz

description: Extract a gzip-compressed tar archive in the current directory
notes: Add -C /path/to/dest to extract to a specific directory. List contents first: tar -tzf <FILENAME>

description: Preview archive contents without extracting
notes: Good practice before extracting to verify paths and avoid directory traversal surprises

description: Show line-by-line differences between two files
notes: Use diff -u for unified format (easier to read). diff -r compares two directories recursively

description: Output the lines of a file in alphabetical order (does not modify the file)
notes: sort -n for numeric order; sort -k2 to sort by second column; sort -u to deduplicate

description: Create a soft (symbolic) link — a pointer to another file or directory
notes: Hard links (ln without -s) cannot span filesystems and cannot point to directories

description: Mirror a directory, copying only changed files (efficient incremental backup)
notes: Add --delete to remove files from DEST that no longer exist in SOURCE. Use -n (dry run) to preview changes

description: Print lines in a file that match a pattern
notes: Use grep -rn '<PATTERN>' . to search all files recursively. grep -c counts matches per file

description: Search all files in the current directory tree for a pattern, showing file and line number
notes: Add --include='*.py' to restrict to specific file types: grep -rn '<PATTERN>' . --include='*.py'

description: Show filesystem disk space usage in human-readable format for all mounted filesystems
variations: how much disk space do i have
how much free space is left
notes: Check 'Use%' column — above 90% is concerning. df -h / shows only the root filesystem

description: Show summarized disk usage for each item in the current directory
notes: du -sh */ shows only subdirectories. Sort by size: du -sh * | sort -rh

description: Identify the 10 largest files and directories under the current path
variations: what files take the most space
notes: Pipe through head -n 20 to see more results

description: List all disk partitions and their sizes (requires sudo)
notes: lsblk is a cleaner tree view of block devices and partitions without requiring sudo

description: List all running processes with PID, CPU%, memory%, and command
notes: ps aux | grep <NAME> to filter by process name. For a live view, use 'top' or 'htop'

description: Interactive real-time view of CPU, memory, and process activity
notes: Press q to quit, k to kill a process, M to sort by memory, P to sort by CPU. 'htop' is a friendlier alternative if installed

description: Send SIGTERM (graceful stop) to a process by its PID

flags.-9: SIGKILL — force kill, cannot be ignored

notes: Get PID from 'ps aux' or 'pgrep <NAME>'. Use kill -9 only if the process ignores SIGTERM

description: Send SIGTERM to all processes matching a name pattern
notes: pkill -9 <NAME> force-kills. killall <NAME> kills by exact name. Use pgrep <NAME> first to preview which PIDs would be affected

description: Start a command and immediately return the shell prompt, running the job in the background
notes: Use 'jobs' to list background jobs; 'fg' to bring last background job to foreground; 'bg' to resume a stopped job

description: Execute a shell script using bash
notes: Alternatively: chmod +x <FILENAME> && ./<FILENAME>. Use 'bash -x <FILENAME>' for debug tracing

description: Display all network interfaces and their assigned IP addresses
notes: ip addr show eth0 for a specific interface. 'hostname -I' prints just the primary IP

description: List all TCP and UDP ports currently listening for connections
notes: ss -tuln is the modern replacement for netstat. Add -p flag (with sudo) to see which process owns each port: ss -tulnp

description: Display the kernel IP routing table
notes: ip route replaces the deprecated 'route -n'. Look for the 'default' line to find your gateway

description: Send 4 ICMP echo packets to a host to test reachability and round-trip time
notes: Without -c the command runs forever — always specify -c for scripts. Some hosts block ICMP; use curl or nc to test HTTP connectivity instead

description: Confirm internet connectivity by pinging a well-known public host
notes: Also test DNS: ping 8.8.8.8 (IP). If IP works but domain doesn't, DNS is the issue

description: Show the network hops from your machine to a remote host
notes: tracepath is an alternative that doesn't require sudo. Useful for diagnosing where packets are being dropped

description: Query DNS to resolve a domain name to its IP address(es)
notes: dig <DOMAIN> gives more detailed output. dig +short <DOMAIN> returns only the IPs

description: Clear the systemd-resolved DNS cache to force fresh lookups
notes: On older systems: /etc/init.d/nscd restart. Verify flush: systemd-resolve --statistics

description: Download a file from a URL to the current directory
notes: curl -O <URL> is an alternative. Use wget -c to resume interrupted downloads

description: Download from a URL and save to a specific filename
notes: curl equivalent: curl -o <FILENAME> <URL>

description: List all active iptables firewall rules (requires sudo)
notes: iptables -L -v shows packet/byte counters. On systems using ufw: 'ufw status verbose'

description: Print all system information: kernel version, hostname, architecture, OS
variations: what operating system am i using
notes: cat /etc/os-release gives distro-specific info (Ubuntu version, etc.)

description: Print only the running kernel version string
notes: Useful when checking if a kernel update requires a reboot

description: Show how long the system has been running, number of users, and load averages
variations: how long has the system been running
how long is the system up
notes: Load averages above number-of-CPUs suggest the system is overloaded

description: Print the machine's hostname
notes: hostnamectl gives full static/transient/pretty names. Set hostname: hostnamectl set-hostname <NAME>

description: Display CPU architecture, cores, threads, speed, and cache information
notes: nproc prints just the number of available CPUs — useful in scripts

description: Display total, used, free, and available RAM and swap in human-readable format
variations: how much memory do i have
notes: The 'available' column is more meaningful than 'free' — it estimates memory usable by new processes

description: Gracefully reboot the machine (requires sudo)
notes: Warns logged-in users if any. Use 'shutdown -r now' as equivalent

description: Gracefully halt and power off the machine immediately (requires sudo)
notes: shutdown -h +5 schedules a shutdown in 5 minutes with a broadcast warning to users

description: Show block devices (disks, partitions) in a tree layout
notes: lsblk -f shows filesystem types and mount points. Good for verifying which disk is which before partitioning

description: List all USB buses and connected devices
notes: lsusb -v for verbose output. lspci lists PCI devices (graphics cards, network adapters)

description: Print all environment variables set in the current shell session
notes: printenv <VAR> prints a single variable. echo $HOME prints $HOME specifically

description: Print the path of the current user's home directory
notes: Also accessible via: cd ~ && pwd. $HOME is set at login from /etc/passwd

description: Define an environment variable in the current shell and export it to child processes
notes: To persist across sessions, add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.profile. Unset with: unset <KEY>

description: View logs from systemd's journal (all services, kernel, boot)
notes: journalctl -u nginx -f follows nginx logs live. journalctl --since '1 hour ago' filters by time

description: Show all loaded systemd service units and their current state
notes: Add --state=failed to see only failed services

description: Start a systemd service (requires sudo)
notes: systemctl start does not persist across reboots — use 'enable' to auto-start on boot

description: Stop a running systemd service (requires sudo)
Stop and start a service to apply new configuration
notes: systemctl reload <NAME> sends SIGHUP (applies config without full restart) — preferred for nginx, apache2

description: Show current state, recent log output, and PID of a systemd service
notes: Exit code 0 = active, 1 = inactive, 3 = not found/dead. Useful in scripts: systemctl is-active <NAME>

description: Configure a service to start automatically on system boot
notes: systemctl enable --now <NAME> enables AND starts immediately in one command

description: Remove a service from the boot startup sequence
Refresh the local list of available packages from all configured repositories
notes: Run this before apt install or apt upgrade to ensure you get the latest versions

description: Install available upgrades for all installed packages
notes: Run 'apt update' first. Use 'apt full-upgrade' to also handle dependency changes and package removals

description: Download and install a package and its dependencies
notes: Use apt install -y for scripted/automated installs. Check package name first with: apt search <NAME>

description: Uninstall a package, keeping its configuration files
notes: apt purge <NAME> also removes config files. apt autoremove cleans up orphaned dependencies

description: Search the package index for packages matching a keyword
notes: apt-cache show <NAME> gives a detailed description before installing

description: Print the username of the currently logged-in user
notes: id prints username plus UID, GID, and group memberships

description: Show who is logged in, their terminal, login time, and what they are running
notes: 'who' is simpler (just name + terminal + time). 'last' shows login history

description: Display a list of recent logins, reboots, and logouts from /var/log/wtmp
notes: last <USERNAME> filters to one user. lastb shows failed login attempts (requires sudo)

description: Create a new user account with a home directory (requires sudo)
notes: Follow with 'passwd <USERNAME>' to set a password. adduser (Debian) is a friendlier interactive alternative

description: Remove a user account and their home directory (requires sudo)

flags.-r: also remove home directory and mail spool

notes: Omit -r to keep the home directory

description: Change the current user's password interactively
notes: sudo passwd <USERNAME> changes another user's password

description: Append a user to a supplementary group without removing existing group memberships

flags.-a: append (do not remove from other groups)

notes: User must log out and back in for the group change to take effect. Verify with: groups <USERNAME>

description: List all groups the current user belongs to
notes: groups <USERNAME> shows groups for a specific user. id shows groups with GIDs

description: Clear the terminal screen (scroll buffer is preserved — you can scroll up)
notes: Ctrl+L does the same. 'reset' also reinitializes the terminal (useful after garbled output)

description: Print a numbered list of previously executed commands from ~/.bash_history
notes: history | grep <KEYWORD> searches history. !<NUMBER> re-runs a command by its history number

description: Filter the command history for entries matching a keyword
notes: Ctrl+R in the shell triggers reverse-incremental-search through history interactively

description: Print the current system date and time
notes: date '+%Y-%m-%d' for ISO format only. date '+%H:%M:%S' for time only

description: Display a calendar for the current month
notes: cal 2025 shows the full year. cal 3 2025 shows March 2025

description: Print a string to stdout
notes: echo -n suppresses the trailing newline. printf offers more formatting options

description: Count how many items (files and directories) are in the current directory
notes: ls -la | wc -l includes the 'total' header line — subtract 1 for accuracy

description: Open the current user's crontab in the default editor to add or modify scheduled tasks
notes: Cron format: min hour day month weekday command. Example: '0 2 * * * /backup.sh' runs at 2am daily

description: Print the current user's scheduled cron jobs
notes: sudo crontab -l -u <USER> lists another user's crontab

description: List all files opened by any process on the system (requires sudo for full view)
notes: lsof -i :80 shows what is listening on port 80. lsof +D /path shows files open in a directory

description: Trace and print all system calls made by a command — useful for debugging crashes or permission issues
notes: strace -p <PID> attaches to a running process. -e trace=file to filter to file-related calls only
